intro: For the one who inspires me the most.

poems.title: The Patio
poems.body:
I want to sit on my patio listening to the wind,
As I rock in my chair thinking about the woman I would never find.
I pour a glass of 20 year old Burgundy into my lonely glass,
As it swirls I can't help but think about how the time will pass.
What good is this moment and how meaningful is my life,
If I can't share these cherry notes I am enjoying if I don't have a wife.
The clouds turn grey and the leaves on the trees howl even louder,
The calm I was sitting in all of a sudden begins to scatter.
I must make the changes to fend off this loneliness,
I must put myself out there to find my happiness.
The next day brings a golden sky in the valley,
I pack my bag and head down a new alley.
As I sit down at a wine bar full of song and dance that I had never explored,
I scan the room expecting the emptiness to which I had so often been accustomed.
I rise out of my chair as my heart unexpectedly comes to despair,
My gaze locks on this maiden with shamrock green eyes and auburn hair.
I firmly plant myself back on my seat,
I try not to stare but I can't help but feel the weight of this feat.
I sat alone and quiet amongst the sound of music,
Hoping she would find me in the loudness of the acoustic.
I raised my hand to order another drink,
As the maiden caught my eyes she raised two fingers and gave me a wink.
Before the waitress had even poured the round,
This maiden was sitting across from me as if to say look what I have found.
Though the atmosphere around was chaotic and vibrant,
Our conversation was mild and full of wonderment.
My glass was no longer alone and full of despair,
It was alive and accompanied as the aromas of our Sancerre.
We were lost in ourselves and in infatuation,
Giving each other the eye to take this moment to fruition.
I raised my hand and paid the tab,
We slowly walked out of the establishment hand in hand.
We wandered all the way to my place,
Never had such a wonderful maiden filled my space.
Now I sit on my patio listening to the wind,
Knowing immediately she was one of a kind.

poems.title: The Train
poems.body:
I want to ride on a slow moving train,
Watching the blue sky turn grey and the grey turn to rain.
The drops trickle down the window the same way they do on my wine glass as I swirl it,As if they were slow moving tears.
I look out into the dawn and think when will I face my fears.
The world through this moving glass picture is full of hope and wonder,
Yet the tiny room I travel in, my loneliness is the only thing I ponder.
What good is this journey if it's a one way ticket for one,
The emptiness I feel in my soul, even with a glass of wine in hand, is next to none.
As the sun hits the green vineyards, a light also hits my heart,
I lift up from the musty cloth bench and walk over to the dining cart.
Amongst the bustling patrons there sits the fairest maiden as the one I often see in my dream,
Her hair as auburn as the turning leaves and eyes that even the leaves on the vines would envy for they are so green.
Even with the rattling of the train her seat across is empty and silent,
She sits with bubbly in hand as if she was waiting for me to fill the silence in that very moment.
We lock eyes and I sit without any fear,
Even with the faint sound of our glasses embracing, we both sit in the silence that only we can hear.
The quiet turns into a whisper and whispers into longing,
We didn't care where we had been or even where we were going.
We want to experience life on a slow moving train,
Where our love will grow and never again will we feel pain.

poems.title: The Cobble Stone Street
poems.body:
I want to walk on a cobblestone street,Where the stones wobble as I steady my feet.
The narrow paths lead to endless turns,I make a left on a shadowed lane where candlelight burns.
In the randomness of my journey lies my fate,
I walk down the lane knowing I will find my date.
There the fair maiden sits with auburn hair and emerald eyes,
Sitting at a sidewalk café, drinking her favorite wines.
Our eyes lock, her gaze shows no hesitation,
I take the creaky chair across from her, knowing I need no permission.
The conversation starts with the clink of a glass,
It continues with every deepening glance.
Not a word is said for the first precious moments,
Words are overrated when all you need is each other's presence.
At last, we take in each other's voices,
And we still haven't stopped, as our life is full of endless stories.
The evening continues in darkness,
And our love grows into a fortress.
The lights dim, the neighborhood quietens,
But our infatuation and passion only livens.
This is the soul mate I have always sought,
She has locked me in her heart's vault.
I want to sit forever on this cobblestone street,
Where the stones wobble, but I will steady my seat.

poems.title: The Vine
poems.body:
I want to grow old like a fine wine and the root of a deep vine,
Where someone turns me every so often and I find myself stretching to the depth of the earth fighting for more time.
Though what's the point of growing old if there is no one to join me on the journey,
I am no longer the callous man I used to be but no one will forgive me.
Vintage after vintage I celebrate the new harvest alone,
I wish someone was there to pick me at the end of every growing season.
When this fall comes I must change this equation,
I must rally the town around me and let them know I'm no longer the man who used to be full of frustration.
I ride into town with nothing but a ton of hope but also a bit of fear,
I scan the townspeople's eyes as I rode my horse and buggy, I realized no one for me had a care.
As the ton of hope turned to full despair and I made my final U-turn,
I caught the glance of the fairest maiden with field green eyes and hair like the harvest, so auburn.
She didn't turn away but instead locked into the depths of my soul,
Could this really be happening, someone willing to look past my past aggression, or was she simply looking at my young foal.
As if possessed and without any control I found myself stopping in front of her,
To my surprise as the entire town shook in disapproval she boarded my chariot amongst all the disapproving laughter.
All of a sudden the laughter and chatter dissipated as our two gazes locked as one,
She held out her hand and our fingers locked with alternating colors of our skin tone.
We fled off into the fields as if this was our annual ritual,
Finally we had found each other and no harvest would ever be so trivial.
I want to grow old like a fine wine and the root of a deep vine,
We both now take turns turning each other and hand in hand we both enjoy our time.
